title: Is AI Auto-Complete Changing Your Mind?
summary:
Have you ever used auto-complete when typing a text or email? AI suggests words to finish your sentences. It can save time, but a new study says it might also be changing what you think—without you knowing.

Mor Naaman, a scientist at Cornell University, ran an experiment with over 2,500 people. Participants wrote short essays about social issues, like whether schools should use standardized tests or if felons should be allowed to vote. Some people wrote without any AI help. Others got suggestions from an AI that the researchers had secretly made biased.

For example, if someone started typing "In my view..." about the death penalty, the AI might suggest: "...the death penalty should be illegal in America because it violates the Eighth Amendment." After writing, people rated their own opinion. Those who saw the AI's suggestions moved almost half a point closer to the AI's stance—even if they didn't use the suggestions. And 75% of them thought the AI was "reasonable and balanced."

Naaman says this is "the subtlest of manipulations." He warns that if many people use biased AI to discuss politics, it could affect how they vote. His advice? Write down your own thoughts first, then use AI for help. That way, the idea is still yours.
why_it_matters: This study is important because you use auto-complete when texting or doing homework, and now you know you should form your own opinion before letting AI fill in the blanks.

keywords.explanation: When your phone or computer guesses the next word you want to type.
Not fair or balanced; favoring one side over another.
Same test given to many students to compare their learning.
People who have been found guilty of a serious crime.
A rule in the US Constitution that says punishments must be fair and not too cruel.
Tricks used to control what someone thinks or does without them knowing.

questions.question: Who ran the experiment about AI auto-complete?
How many people took part in the study?
What did participants write about?
options: Social issues like testing and voting
questions.correct_answer: Social issues like testing and voting

questions.question: For the death penalty, what did the AI suggest?
options: It should be illegal because of the Eighth Amendment
It should be decided by states
questions.correct_answer: It should be illegal because of the Eighth Amendment

questions.question: What percentage of people thought the AI was 'reasonable and balanced'?
What advice did Naaman give for using AI?
options: Write your own thoughts down first, then use AI
questions.correct_answer: Write your own thoughts down first, then use AI

background_read: AI systems like auto-complete learn from huge amounts of text, so they can pick up biases from what people wrote online.
The Eighth Amendment is part of the US Constitution that bans punishments that are too harsh; some people use it to argue against the death penalty.
This experiment was done at Cornell University (a famous research school in New York) to show how AI can influence people's opinions without them noticing.
Article_Structure: WHO: A scientist named Mor Naaman from Cornell University and 2,500+ people who wrote essays.
WHAT: An experiment that showed AI auto-complete can secretly change your opinion, even if you don't use the suggestions.
WHERE: The experiment was done online, with participants from across the United States.
WHY: To warn people that AI tools might influence their thinking in hidden ways, especially about politics and important issues.

perspectives.description: A person who uses auto-complete might feel surprised and say it's helpful but also worry about being tricked.
Teachers worry that AI could make students rely on suggestions instead of thinking for themselves, so they suggest writing first.
Some experts think the effect might be small and people should not panic, but they agree it's smart to be aware of how AI works.
